votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, checking the length of the list and verifying that each element matches the expected factorial or sum based on whether its 1-based index is even or odd.
The Python code correctly translates the Coq specification, checking the length of the list and verifying each element against the factorial or sum formula based on the parity of its 1-based index.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, checking the length of the list and verifying that each element at index i-1 matches the factorial of i if i is even, or the sum from 1 to i if i is odd.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec: it checks that the list length equals n, and for each i from 1 to n, verifies that the element at index i-1 equals factorial(i) if i is even, or the sum 1+...+i (computed as i*(i+1)//2) if i is odd. The factorial and sum computations match the Coq definitions (fact and Nat.div with integer division), and the even check is equivalent.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec: it checks that the list has length n, and for each i from 1 to n, the element at index i-1 equals factorial(i) if i is even, or the sum 1+...+i (computed as i*(i+1)//2) if i is odd. The helper functions (fact, sum_nat, even) match the Coq definitions (fact, Nat.div formula, even), and the iteration logic correctly mirrors the Coq forall quantifier for concrete inputs.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. It checks that the list length equals n, and for each i from 1 to n, verifies that the element at index i-1 equals factorial(i) if i is even, or sum(1..i) = i*(i+1)/2 if i is odd. The factorial and sum computations match the Coq definitions (fact and the Gauss formula), and the even check (i % 2 == 0) matches Coq's even predicate.
Both check length equals n and for each i from 1 to n set l[i-1] to fact(i) if i is even, else (i*(i+1))//2. Handles n=0 and uses exact integer division; behavior matches on all natural inputs.
Checks length n and for each i=1..n compares l[i-1] to factorial(i) if i even, else (i*(i+1))//2; matches Coq spec including 1-based indexing and Gauss sum.
Python checks length and for each i=1..n sets l[i-1] to fact(i) if i is even else (i*(i+1))//2, matching Coq’s spec (Nat.div on an even product). Indexing, parity, and edge case n=0 align.
